# RFC-0001: Benchmark execution and assessment model

> **Version:** 0.2.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** stable

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0001:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0001c-summary"></a>

This RFC defines the execution and assessment model for `agent-skill-bench`.

**Scope:** benchmark suites and cases, case resolution, runtime execution, deterministic assessment, optional judge assessment, and normalized run artifacts.

**Rationale:** the framework needs a stable behavioral model that is independent of any single benchmark suite or runtime adapter and can be verified consistently across implementations.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0001:C-SUITE-AND-CASE-RESOLUTION] Suite and case resolution (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-suite-and-case-resolution"></a>

A benchmark execution MUST resolve one suite definition and one case definition into a single resolved case before invoking any runtime.
The suite MUST define the benchmark-wide defaults that the framework needs for execution and assessment, including execution defaults and any prompt or assessment contracts that apply to the suite.
A case MAY override suite defaults where the framework explicitly permits overrides.
The resolved case MUST preserve the selected suite ID, case ID, mode, execution kind, source path, and the effective policies that will govern execution and assessment.

**Rationale:** benchmark runs need a reproducible pre-execution representation so runtime behavior and later assessment can be explained against a single effective configuration.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-SUITE-OWNED-SEMANTICS] Suite-owned semantics (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-suite-owned-semantics"></a>

The shared benchmark framework MUST interpret suite-owned contracts and policies generically rather than embedding benchmark-domain semantics in framework code.
A suite MUST be able to define its own prompt contract, deterministic rules, and judge policy without requiring the framework to hardcode domain-specific section names, anti-pattern phrases, or rubric language.
A runtime adapter MUST NOT become the authority for suite semantics.

**Rationale:** the framework is shared across skill domains, while the meaning of benchmark expectations belongs to the suite that defines them.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-RUNTIME-EXECUTION] Runtime execution model (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-runtime-execution"></a>

Candidate execution MUST compile a resolved case into a runtime request and execute it through a shared agent-runtime abstraction.
Judge execution MUST compile a judge task into the same shared agent-runtime abstraction rather than inventing a separate vendor-specific execution model.
A runtime adapter MUST return the candidate or judge output text on successful completion.
When a runtime request includes structured-output constraints, the runtime adapter MUST either return parsed structured output that satisfies those constraints or fail the run.
The runtime abstraction MUST be capable of carrying execution intent such as purpose, working directory controls, skill bindings, timeout controls, and optional structured-output constraints.
A runtime adapter SHOULD surface failures in a form that preserves the failing stage and raw evidence needed for later normalization.

**Rationale:** candidate and judge runs differ in purpose, but they share the same class of runtime behavior and therefore should be modeled uniformly. Structured judge execution is not optional once the request asks for validated structured output, and preserving raw failure evidence is necessary for stable failure classification later in the pipeline.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-ASSESSMENT-AND-ARTIFACTS] Assessment and run artifacts (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-assessment-and-artifacts"></a>

Each attempted benchmark execution MUST yield a normalized run artifact, including attempts that fail before deterministic assessment can run.
A benchmark execution that produces candidate output text MUST produce a deterministic rule assessment, even when no judge runtime is configured.
A benchmark execution MAY produce a judge assessment when a judge runtime is configured and judge execution completes successfully.
A normalized run artifact MUST include enough stable information to identify the executed case, the selected runtime and policy context, the candidate runtime outcome, the candidate output when one was produced, deterministic assessment results when they were produced, and any judge assessment that was produced.
When deterministic checks fail, the artifact MUST preserve stable failure-mode codes derived from the failed checks.
When candidate or judge execution fails, the artifact MUST preserve stable runtime-failure classification data separately from deterministic failure-mode codes.

**Rationale:** regression tracking depends on a persistent, comparable artifact that records both the generated output and the framework's interpretation of the run. Once runtime failures are part of the artifact contract, reporting and reevaluation can reason about failed attempts without scraping adapter-specific exception text.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-RUNTIME-FAILURE-OUTCOMES] Runtime failure outcomes (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-runtime-failure-outcomes"></a>

The framework MUST classify runtime failures with stable outcome codes rather than treating adapter-specific exception text as the contract.
The stable outcome taxonomy MUST distinguish at least `runtime_timeout`, `structured_output_mismatch`, `runtime_transport_failure`, and `workspace_materialization_failure`.
A failed candidate run MUST record the candidate runtime outcome code and a human-readable summary in the normalized artifact.
A failed judge run MUST record the judge runtime outcome code and a human-readable summary in the normalized artifact without rewriting the candidate runtime outcome.
The framework MAY define additional stable outcome codes, but it MUST preserve the meaning of the required codes across runtime adapters.

**Rationale:** the current adapters already fail in materially different ways, including timeout exceptions, structured-output validation failures, runtime invocation failures, and workspace preparation failures. Stable outcome codes let reports and future automation compare these failures without coupling to Codex CLI stderr, Claude SDK exceptions, or Python exception class names.

*Since: v0.2.0*

---

## Changelog

### v0.2.0 (2026-04-07)

Add runtime failure outcomes and attempted-run artifact requirements

#### Added

- add runtime failure outcome taxonomy for candidate and judge runs
- require normalized artifacts for attempted runs and runtime failures
